human: She sank into the hot clamor of the shoeshine boys and the bird sellers, the hawkers of cheap books and the witch doctors and the sellers of sweets who shouted over the din of the crowd: pineapple sweets for your sweetie, coconut candy is dandy, brown-sugar loaf for your sugar. But, indifferent to the uproar, she was captivated on the spot by a paper seller who was demonstrating magic inks, red inks with an ambience of blood, inks of sad aspect for messages of condolence, phosphorescent inks for reading in the dark, invisible inks that revealed themselves in the light. She wanted all of them so she could amuse Florentino Ariza and astound him with her wit, but after several trials she decided on a bottle of gold ink. Then she went to the candy sellers sitting behind their big round jars and she bought six of each kind, pointing at the glass because she could not make herself heard over all the shouting: six angel hair, six tinned milk, six sesame seed bars, six cassava pastries, six chocolate bars, six blancmanges, six tidbits of the queen, six of this and six of that, six of everything, and she tossed them into the maid's baskets with an irresistible grace and a complete detachment from the stormclouds of flies on the syrup, from the continual hullabaloo and the vapor of rancid sweat that reverberated in the deadly heat. She was awakened from the spell by a good-natured black woman with a colored cloth around her head who was round and handsome and offered her a triangle of pineapple speared on the tip of a butcher's knife. She took it, she put it whole into her mouth, she tasted it, and was chewing it as her eyes wandered over the crowd, when a sudden shock rooted her on the spot. Behind her, so close to her ear that only she could hear it in the tumult, she heard his voice: "This is not the place for a crowned goddess." She turned her head and saw, a hand's breadth from her eyes, those other glacial eyes, that livid face, those lips petrified with fear, just as she had seen them in the crowd at Midnight Mass the first time he was so close to her, but now, instead of the commotion of love, she felt the abyss of disenchantment. In an instant the magnitude of her own mistake was revealed to her, and she asked herself, appalled, how she could have nurtured such a chimera in her heart for so long and with so much ferocity. She just managed to think: My God, poor man! Florentino Ariza smiled, tried to say something, tried to follow her, but she erased him from her life with a wave of her hand. "No, please," she said to him. "Forget it."
gemini: She plunged into the hot, tumultuous uproar of shoeshine boys and bird sellers, of secondhand booksellers, faith healers, and candy vendors crying out over the din, announcing pineapple coconut cakes for the girls, coconut cakes for the crazy, panela cakes for Micaela. But she was indifferent to the roar, charmed immediately by a stationer who was demonstrating magic writing inks: red inks with the climate of blood, inks with sad overtones for funeral messages, phosphorescent inks for reading in the dark, invisible inks that were revealed in the glow of a fire. She wanted them all to play with Florentino Ariza, to frighten him with her wit, but after several tests, she decided on a small bottle of gold ink. Then she went to the candy vendors sitting behind their large glass jars and bought six sweets of each kind, pointing them out through the glass because she could not make herself heard in the shouting: six cabellitos de angel, six little milk preserves, six sesame bricks, six yucca alfajores, six diabolines, six piononos, six bocaditos de la reina, six of this and six of that, six of everything, and she was tossing them into the maid's baskets with an irresistible grace, completely oblivious to the torment of the clouds of flies over the syrup, oblivious to the constant havoc, oblivious to the stench of rancid sweat that reverberated in the deadly heat. A happy black woman with a colorful cloth on her head, round and beautiful, woke her from the spell by offering her a triangle of pineapple skewered on the tip of a butcher knife. She took it, put the whole thing in her mouth, savored it, and was savoring it with her eyes wandering over the crowd, when a commotion planted her in her spot. Behind her, so close to her ear that only she could hear it in the uproar, she had heard the voice: "This is not a good place for a crowned goddess." She turned her head and saw, two spans from her eyes, the other icy eyes, the livid face, the lips petrified with fear, just as she had seen them in the commotion of the midnight mass the first time he was so close to her, but unlike then, she did not feel the commotion of love but the abyss of disappointment. In an instant, the magnitude of her own deception was revealed to her completely, and she wondered, terrified, how she could have nurtured such a chimera in her heart for so long and with such cruelty. She barely managed to think: "My God, poor man!" Florentino Ariza smiled, tried to say something, tried to follow her, but she erased him from her life with a gesture of her hand. "No, please," she told him. "Forget it."
claude:
She plunged into the warm hubbub of the shoeshine boys and bird sellers, the secondhand booksellers and healers and the sweet-sellers who called out above the din: coconut sweets for the coconut lovers, pineapple cocadas for the girls, brown-sugar ones for Micaela. But she was indifferent to the uproar, immediately captivated by a stationer who was demonstrating magical writing inks — red inks with the warmth of blood, inks with sad undertones for funeral messages, phosphorescent inks for reading in the dark, invisible inks that revealed themselves in the glow of firelight. She wanted them all, to play games with Florentino Ariza, to dazzle him with her cleverness, but after several tests she settled on a small bottle of gold ink. Then she went to the sweet-sellers sitting behind their great glass jars, and bought six sweets of each kind, pointing to them through the glass because she could not make herself heard above the shouting: six angel-hair sweets, six milk conserves, six sesame-seed bricks, six yuca alfajores, six diabolines, six piononos, six queen's morsels, six of this and six of that, six of everything, dropping them into the servant girl's baskets with an irresistible grace, utterly oblivious to the torment of the clouds of flies hovering over the syrup, oblivious to the constant commotion, oblivious to the reek of stale sweat shimmering in the deadly heat.

A happy Black woman with a colourful cloth wrapped around her head — round and beautiful — broke the spell, offering her a triangle of pineapple speared on the tip of a butcher's knife. She took it, put the whole piece in her mouth, savoured it, and was savouring it still with her gaze wandering over the crowd, when a tremor rooted her to the spot. Close behind her, so near her ear that only she could hear it above the tumult, she heard the voice: "This is no place for a crowned goddess."

She turned her head and saw, just two hands' breadths from her eyes, those other glacial eyes, the ashen face, the lips petrified with fear — just as she had seen them in the crush at Midnight Mass the very first time he had been so close to her — but unlike that occasion, she felt not the shock of love but the abyss of disenchantment. In an instant the full magnitude of her own self-deception was revealed to her, and she asked herself in terror how she could have nurtured such a chimera in her heart for so long, and with such cruelty. She had barely time to think: "My God, poor man!"

Florentino Ariza smiled, tried to say something, tried to follow her, but she erased him from her life with a wave of her hand. "No, please," she said. "Forget it."
